steps.explanation: Multiply the first equation by 5: \(5(7x+4y)=5(3)\) gives \(35x+20y=15\), which is identical to the second equation. Both equations describe the same line \(7x+4y=3\), so every point on that line satisfies both.

A parametrization \((x(r), y(r))\) works for all real \(r\) if substituting into \(7x+4y=3\) always yields 3. Testing choice B with \(x = -\frac{4r}{7}+\frac{3}{7}\) and \(y = r\): \(7\!\left(-\frac{4r}{7}+\frac{3}{7}\right)+4r = -4r+3+4r = 3\). This holds for every \(r\).

Evaluate each choice by substituting into \(7x+4y=3\):
  • Choice A: \(\left(\frac{r}{5}+3,\,-\frac{r}{5}+15\right)\). Compute \(7\!\left(\frac{r}{5}+3\right)+4\!\left(-\frac{r}{5}+15\right)=\frac{7r}{5}+21-\frac{4r}{5}+60=\frac{3r}{5}+81\). This equals 3 only when \(r=-130\), not for all \(r\). Incorrect.
  • Choice B: \(\left(-\frac{4r}{7}+\frac{3}{7},\,r\right)\). Compute \(7\!\left(-\frac{4r}{7}+\frac{3}{7}\right)+4r=-4r+3+4r=3\). True for every \(r\). That matches choice B — B is correct.
  • Choice C: \(\left(r,\,\frac{7r}{4}+\frac{3}{4}\right)\). Compute \(7r+4\!\left(\frac{7r}{4}+\frac{3}{4}\right)=7r+7r+3=14r+3\). This equals 3 only when \(r=0\), not for all \(r\). Incorrect.
  • Choice D: \(\left(r,\,-\frac{4r}{7}+\frac{3}{7}\right)\). Compute \(7r+4\!\left(-\frac{4r}{7}+\frac{3}{7}\right)=7r-\frac{16r}{7}+\frac{12}{7}=\frac{33r}{7}+\frac{12}{7}\). This equals 3 only when \(r=\frac{3}{11}\), not for all \(r\). Incorrect.
